revision.text:
[[ar:هوريو-جي]][[arz:هوريو جى]][[ast:Hōryū-ji]][[ba:Хорю-дзи]][[bg:Хорю-джи]][[ca:Hōryū-ji]][[ceb:Horyu-ji]][[cs:Hórjúdži]][[de:Hōryū-ji]][[en:Hōryū-ji]][[eo:Hōryūji]][[es:Hōryū-ji]][[eu:Hōryū-ji]][[fa:هوریو-جی]][[fi:Hōryū-ji]][[fr:Hōryū-ji]][[gan:法隆寺]][[he:הוריו-ג'י]][[hr:Horyu-ji]][[hu:Hórjúdzsi]][[hy:Հորյու ձի]][[id:Horyuji]][[is:Hōryū-ji]][[it:Hōryū-ji]][[ja:法隆寺]][[ko:호류지]][[la:Hōryū-ji]][[lt:Horiūdži]][[ml:ഹോര്യൂ-ജി]][[ms:Hōryū-ji]][[nl:Horyu-ji]][[pl:Hōryū-ji]][[pt:Horyu-ji]][[ro:Hōryū-ji]][[ru:Хорю-дзи]][[scn:Hōryū-ji]][[sh:Horju-dži]][[sk:Hórjúdži]][[sl:Horju-dži]][[sr:Horjudži]][[sv:Horyu-ji]][[th:โฮรีวจิ]][[tr:Hōryū-ji]][[uk:Монастир Хорю]][[vi:Chùa Hōryū]][[war:Hōryū-ji]][[wuu:法隆寺]][[zh-yue:法隆寺]][[zh:法隆寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Horyu-ji45s2s4500.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Hossō-shū|qid=Q124667153|1=Hossō-shū}}; {{ill|Shōtoku-shū|qid=Q11611002|1=Shōtoku-shū}}|prefecture={{ill|Hōryūji|qid=Q11555387|1=Hōryūji}}; {{ill|Ikaruga|qid=Q379233|1=Ikaruga, Nara}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.614444444444445|N|135.73416666666665|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|1=Prince Shōtoku}}; {{ill|Suiko|qid=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}|part_of={{ill|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|qid=Q1333799|1=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area}}; {{ill|Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q11611000|1=Historical sites of Prince Shōtoku}}; {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|qid=Q1131210|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|seven great temples founded by Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q11610998|1=seven great temples founded by Prince Shōtoku}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Hōryū-ji
法隆寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Horyu-ji National Treasure World heritage 国宝・世界遺産法隆寺85.JPG | alt = | caption = Kondo (left), and Pagoda (right) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=220}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 1-1 Hōryū-ji Sannai, {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|qid=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga-chō|1=Ikaruga, Nara}}, {{ill|Ikoma District, Nara|en|Ikoma District, Nara|qid=Q971003|lt=Ikoma-gun|1=Ikoma District, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}} | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.horyuji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} | year_completed = 607 }} {{JapaneseBuddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Hōryū-ji'''|法隆寺|''Temple of the Flourishing {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}''|lead=yes}} is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} temple that was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|qid=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|qid=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga|1=Ikaruga, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its full name is {{nihongo|''Hōryū Gakumonji''|法隆学問寺}}, or Learning Temple of the Flourishing Law, the complex serving as both a seminary and monastery. The temple was founded by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} in 607, but according to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', in 670 all buildings were burned down by lightning. However, reconstructed at least 1,300 years ago, the Kondō (main hall) is widely recognized as the world's oldest wooden building.{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/660|title=Buddhist Monuments in the Horyu-ji Area, UNESCO World Heritage|access-date=2007-04-02}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D7_MSioiABQC&q=horyu+ji&pg=PA47|title=Gateway to Japan, "A Japanese Prince and his temple"|access-date=2007-04-02|publisher=Kodansha International|year=1998|author1=June Kinoshita |author2=Nicholas Palevsky |isbn=9784770020185}} A fire that broke out during the dismantling and repair of the Kondō on January 26, 1949 destroyed a mural of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, a national treasure, and shocked the Japanese. Based on this accident, the day when the fire broke out is now fire prevention day for cultural properties. In 1993, Hōryū-ji Temple, along with [[Hokki-ji]], was registered as Japan's first [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} under the name of ''{{ill|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|en|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|qid=Q1333799|lt=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|1=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area}}''. A tree ring survey conducted in 2001 revealed that the {{ill|Shinbashira|en|Shinbashira|qid=Q11583259|lt=Shinbashira|1=Shinbashira}} of the five-story {{ill|Pagoda|en|Pagoda|qid=Q199451|lt=Pagoda|1=Pagoda}} were cut down in 594, before it burned down in 670.{{cite book| title=法隆寺五重塔心柱の年輪年代 | url=https://repository.nabunken.go.jp/dspace/bitstream/11177/309/1/BA67898227_2001_032_033.pdf| last=ja:光谷拓実| author-link=:ja:光谷拓実| year=2001| access-date=2020-02-08}} == History == [[File:Horyu-ji45s2s4500.jpg|thumb|250px|left|[[Shichidō garan]]]] The temple was originally commissioned by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}; at the time it was called Wakakusadera, a name that is still sometimes used.{{cite book |last1=Mason |first1=Penelope |title=History of Japanese Art |date=2005 |publisher=Pearson |location=Upper Saddle River, NJ |isbn=0-13-117601-3 |pages=61 |edition= Second}} This first temple is believed to have been completed by 607.{{cite book |last1=Buswell |first1=R.E.J. |title=The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism |date=2013 |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton, NJ}} Hōryū-ji was dedicated to {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, the {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=Buddha|1=Buddhahood}} of healing and in honor of the prince's father. Excavations done in 1939 confirmed that Prince Shotoku's palace, the Ikaruga-no-miya ({{Lang|ja|斑鳩宮}}), occupied the eastern part of the current temple complex, where the Tō-in ({{Lang|ja|東院}}) sits today.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mwgfPXK1kC&q=horyu+ji&pg=PA175|title=The Cambridge history of Japan "The Asuka Enlightenment" p.175|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Cambridge University|year=1988|author=John Whitney Hall|isbn=9780521223522}} Also discovered were the ruins of a temple complex which was southwest of the prince's palace and not completely within the present temple complex. The original temple, named by modern historians and archaeologists Wakakusa-[[Shichidō garan|garan]] ({{Lang|ja|若草伽藍}}), was lost, probably burned to the ground after being hit by lightning in 670. The temple was reconstructed but slightly reoriented in a northwest position, which is believed to have been completed by around 711.{{cite web | last = Web Japan | first = sponsored by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Japan | title = One hundred years older than supposed?: World Heritage Pagoda | url=http://web-japan.org/trends00/honbun/tj010330.html | access-date = 2007-04-04 }} The temple was repaired and reassembled in the early twelfth century, in 1374, and 1603.{{cite book | title=Conservation of Historic Timber Structures: an ecological approach |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wVk7s0W8IT8C&pg=PA22| last=Marstein| first=Nils|author2=Knut Einar Larsen| year=2000| page=22| publisher=Elsevier | isbn=978-0-7506-3434-2 }} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, as the cult of Shōtoku rose to prominence in Japan, Hōryū-ji became an important site for veneration of the long-dead prince. Ritual practices dedicated to Prince Shōtoku increased in number during this time. A memorial service for the prince called the ceremony of Shōryō-e became an annual event at Hōryū-ji in the early 12th century, and it is still practiced at the temple and other temples associated with Prince Shōtoku to this day. The Kamakura and early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} also brought new additions to Hōryū-ji, including the dedication of several new halls in the Eastern and Western compounds to venerate the Prince as the incarnation of the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} [[Kannon]].{{cite journal |last1=Pradel |first1=Chari |title=Shōkō Mandara and the Cult of Prince Shōtoku in the Kamakura Period |journal=Artibus Asiae |date=2008 |volume=68 |issue=2 |pages=215–246 |doi=10.61342/JEFQ7956 |jstor=40599600 }} The growth of the Shōtoku cult from the 7th century onward propelled the rise of Hōryū-ji as a well-known temple in Japan. By the end of Tokugawa rule in the mid-1800s, the temple was receiving extensive funds from the shogunate on a regular basis. Furthermore, the temple grew and maintained close relations with the Hossō sect throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}.{{cite journal |last1=McDermott |first1=Hiroko |title=The Hōryūji Treasures and Early Meiji Cultural Policy |journal=Monumenta Nipponica |date=Autumn 2006 |volume=61 |issue=3 |page=342 |doi=10.1353/mni.2006.0033 |s2cid=143040238 }} Beginning in the early years of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, significant political shifts in Japan brought new challenges for Hōryū-ji. [[Shinto]] was instated as the official state religion in 1868, resulting in government confiscation of many Buddhist lands, strict government supervision and categorization of Buddhist temples, and a steep decrease in financial support for Hōryū-ji itself. The recategorization of officially recognized Buddhist sects by the government, which occurred soon after the start of Meiji rule, did not recognize the Hossō sect as a formal institution of Japanese Buddhism. When the seat of the Hossō sect, [[Kōfuku-ji]], was shut down for a time during the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], Hōryū-ji became affiliated with {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}}.{{sfn|McDermott|2006|p=341}} However, after the government changed its position and allowed Buddhist temples to choose their own affiliated sect in the late 19th century, Hōryū-ji renewed its affiliation with the Hossō school.{{sfn|McDermott|2006|p=341}} Due to the lack of resources during the early Meiji period, the monks at Hōryū-ji decided to donate many of the temple's treasures for museum display.{{sfn|McDermott|2006|p=362}} They were able to secure compensation for this donation, improving the financial situation of the temple. Conservation work at the site began in 1895, but culminated in 1934, when a massive restoration project at Hōryū-ji began.{{cite news |last1=Burgess |first1=John |title=After 51 Years, a Temple is Restored |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/1985/12/26/after-51-years-a-temple-is-restored/39e9345f-d796-40be-b639-587fba1d8319/ |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=26 April 2019}} The project was interrupted during the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]], when large portions of the temple itself were dismantled and hidden in the hills surrounding Nara. However, due to the policy of the [[:en:United States of America|United States of America]] regarding the preservation of cultural sites in Nara and Kyoto, the entire site was spared from bombings during the war. The restoration project resumed after the war and concluded in 1985. Much of the temple complex was repaired from centuries of environmental damage. During the restoration, older paintings of the temple were used to determine the original layout of the complex, and many of the living quarters built during the intervening years were demolished. Today, the temple can be identified as the headquarters of the Shōtoku sect, and is a popular site for pilgrimage. As a UNESCO World Heritage Site, Hōryū-ji is also an attractive site for tourists. According to the temple's website, it is currently home to over 180 of Japan's designated National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, and was the first structure in Japan to become a World Heritage Site.{{cite web |title=Introduction: Horyuji |url=http://www.horyuji.or.jp/en/garan/ |website=Horyuji |access-date=27 April 2019}} Hōryū-ji also still holds frequent events in a variety of locations in the complex, and many of its structures are open to the public. == Architecture == [[File:Horyu-ji03s3200.jpg|thumb|right|The ''Chūmon'' (Inner Gate) with its entasis columns]] [[File:Buddhist Monuments in the Horyu-ji Area-122502.jpg|thumb|left|{{ill|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|en|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|qid=Q1333799|lt=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|1=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area}}]] [[File:Buddhist Monuments in the Horyu-ji Area-122508.jpg|thumb|right|Closer look of wooden carving in {{ill|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|en|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|qid=Q1333799|lt=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|1=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area}}.]] === Present complex === The current temple is made up of two areas, the Sai-in ({{Lang|ja|西院}}) in the west and the Tō-in ({{Lang|ja|東院}}) in the east. The western part of the temple contains the Kondō ({{Lang|ja|金堂}}, sanctuary Hall) and the temple's five-story pagoda. The Tō-in area holds the octagonal Yumedono Hall ({{Lang|ja|夢殿}}, Hall of Dreams) and sits 122 meters east of the Sai-in area. The complex also contains monk's quarters, lecture halls, libraries, and dining halls. === Characteristics === [[File:Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|thumb|left|The belfry of the East Precinct]] The reconstructed buildings embrace significant cultural influences from the {{ill|Three Kingdoms of Korea|en|Three Kingdoms of Korea|qid=Q165292|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea|1=Three Kingdoms of Korea}}, particularly those of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]],{{cite book|author=Sameer Das Gupta|title=Advanced history of Buddhism: monasteries and temples|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H4ckAQAAIAAJ&q=H%C5%8Dryu-ji+baekje|date=1 January 2008|publisher=Cyber Tech Publications|pages=289–|isbn=9788178843438}} from {{ill|Eastern Han|en|Eastern Han|qid=Q1147037|lt=Eastern Han|1=Eastern Han}} to {{ill|Northern Wei|en|Northern Wei|qid=Q875305|lt=Northern Wei|1=Northern Wei}} of China,{{cite web|url=https://www.orientalarchitecture.com/nara/horyujiindex.htm|title=Asian Historical Architecture: A Photographic Survey|last=orientalarchitecture.com|website=Asian Architecture}} as well as from {{ill|Ancient Greek architecture|en|Ancient Greek architecture|qid=Q331273|lt=Ancient Greek architecture|1=Ancient Greek architecture}}, especially on the columns.{{Cite journal |last=Inoue |first=Shoichi |date=2000 |title=Interpretation of Ancient Japanese Architecture: Focusing on Links with World History |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/25791051 |journal=Japan Review |issue=12 |pages=129–143 |jstor=25791051 |issn=0915-0986}} The reconstruction has allowed Hōryū-ji to absorb and feature a unique fusion of early Asuka period style elements, added with some distinct ones only seen in Hōryū-ji, such as the extremely small proportions of the fifth story of the pagoda, which buildings constructed in later years lack. It is also home to unique examples of early Japanese architecture, such as the Tamamushi Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Mason |first1=Penelope |title=History of Japanese Art |date=2005 |publisher=Pearson |location=Upper Saddle River, NJ |isbn=0-13-117601-3 |pages=65 |edition= Second}} There are many features that suggest the current precinct of Hōryū-ji is not entirely related to the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} style in the same way as other works from the period.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Gt1jTpXAThwC&pg=PA731|title=Banister Fletcher's A History of Architecture |page=731|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Architectural Press|year=1996|first1=Banister |last1=Fletcher |first2=Dan |last2=Cruickshank|isbn=9780750622677}} Scholars note that the style of Hōryū-ji is more "conservative" than other examples from the period, such as [[:en:Yakushiji|Yakushiji]].{{cite book |last1=Mason |first1=Penelope |title=History of Japanese Art |date=2005 |publisher=Pearson |location=Upper Saddle River, NJ |isbn=0-13-117601-3 |page=62 |edition= Second}} === Pagoda === [[File:Horyu-ji06s3200.jpg|thumb|left|The pagoda has certain characteristics unique to Hōryū-ji]] The five-story pagoda, located in Sai-in area, stands at 32.45 meters in height (122 feet) and is one of the oldest extant wooden buildings in the world. The wood used in the central pillar or axis mundi of the pagoda is estimated through a {{ill|Dendrochronology|en|Dendrochronology|qid=Q80205|lt=dendrochronological|1=Dendrochronology}} analysis to have been felled in 594. The axis mundi rests three meters below the surface of the massive foundation stone, stretching into the ground. At its base, a relic believed to be a fragment of the bones of the Buddha is enshrined. Around it, four sculpted scenes from the life of the Buddha face in the four cardinal directions.{{cite book | title=''sekai bunka isan Horyuji o kataru'' 世界文化遺産法隆寺を語る (Horyuji, a World Heritage described)| last=Ryōshin Takada 高田良信| year=2007| pages=185–190| publisher=Yanagihara Shuppan 柳原出版}} During the reconstruction of the pagoda in the 1950s, the reliquary was shortly removed from the hollow for restoration, then placed back. The pagoda is five-storied but, as is customary for pagodas, there is no access to the interior.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RfswGk-C0ykC&q=horyu+ji&pg=RA3-PA40|title=The Arts of Japan, p40|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Kodansha International|year=2003|author=Seiroku Noma|work=John Rosenfeld (Trans.)|isbn=9784770029775}} === Kondō === [[File:Horyu-ji42s3200.jpg|thumb|right|The ''kondō'']] The {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=kondō|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}, located side by side to the pagoda in Sai-in, is another one of the oldest wood buildings extant in the world. The hall measures 18.5 meters by 15.2 meters. The hall has two stories, with roofs curved in the corners. Only the first story has a double roof. This was added later in the Nara period with extra posts to hold up the original first roof because it extended more than four meters past the building.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cSxV9fhu-CYC&q=horyu+ji&pg=PA20|title=ISBN 0195088913 p20|isbn=9780195088915|last1=Crouch|first1=Dora P|last2=Johnson|first2=June G|year=2001|publisher=Oxford University }} Not retrievable on the web as of April 2nd, 2007 Due to a fire that broke out on January 26, 1949, severe damage was caused to the building, mainly its first floor, and the murals. As a result of the restoration (completed in 1954), it is estimated that about fifteen to twenty percent of the original seventh century Kondo materials is left in the current building, while the charred members were carefully removed and rebuilt to a separate fireproof warehouse for future research.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wVk7s0W8IT8C&pg=PA21|title=Conservation of Historic Timber Structures: an ecological approach p21|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Elsevier|isbn=9780750634342|author1=Nils Marstein|author2=Knut Einar Larsen|author-link1=Nils Marstein|year=2000}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zh88TftnTsYC&pg=RA1-PA48|title=Hozon: architectural and urban conservation in Japan, p48|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Edition Axel Menges|year=1998|author1=Niels Gutschow |author2=Siegfried RCT Enders |isbn=9783930698981}} Through a recent dendrochronological analysis carried out using the materials preserved during the restorations done in the 1950s, it has turned out that some of them were felled prior to 670, suggesting a possibility that the current kondō was already under construction when "the fire in 670", as recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'', burned the former Wakakusa-garan down.{{Cite web|date=2004-07-20|title=Find rekindles debate over Horyuji Temple|url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2004/07/20/national/find-rekindles-debate-over-horyuji-temple/|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190107222820if_/https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2004/07/20/national/find-rekindles-debate-over-horyuji-temple/#.XDPSot-nxPY|archive-date=2019-01-07|access-date=2021-08-14|website=[[Japan Times]]}} The hall holds the famous Shaka Triad, together with a bronze Yakushi and Amida Nyorai statues, and other national treasures. The wall paintings shown today in the Kondō are a reproduction from 1967. [[File:HoryujiYumedono0363edit4.jpg|thumb|220px|Yumedono, a hall associated with Prince Shōtoku]] ===Yumedono (Hall of Dreams)=== Yumedono is one of the main constructions in the Tō-in area, built on the ground which was once Prince Shōtoku's private palace, {{Lang|ja-latn|Ikaruga no miya}}. The present incarnation of this hall was built in 739 to assuage the Prince's spirit. The hall acquired its present-day common name in the Heian period, after a legend that says a Buddha arrived as Prince Shōtoku and meditated in a hall that existed here. The hall also contains the famous Yumedono Kannon (also Kuse-, or Guze Kannon); which is only displayed at certain times of the year.Usually in spring and autumn, for about a month period each. == Treasures == The treasures of the temple are considered to be a time capsule of Buddhist art from the sixth and seventh century. Many of the frescoes, statues, and other pieces of art within the temple, as well as the architecture of the temple's buildings themselves show the strong cultural influence from China, Korea and India, as well as aspects of Buddhist practice in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://scienceview.berkeley.edu/VI/handbooks_archaeology.html |title=The Archeology of Korea and Cultural Features |access-date=2007-04-02 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070713164925/http://scienceview.berkeley.edu/VI/handbooks_archaeology.html |archive-date=2007-07-13 }}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mwgfPXK1kC&q=koguryo+murals&pg=PA176|title=The Cambridge history of Japan "The Asuka Enlightenment" p.176|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Cambridge University|year=1988|author=John Whitney Hall|isbn=9780521223522}} The Tokyo National Museum holds over 300 objects which were donated to the Imperial Household by Hōryū-ji in 1878. Some of these items are on public display, and all are available for study as part of the museum's digital collection.{{cite web|url=http://www.tnm.go.jp/en/servlet/Con?pageId=B01&processId=00&mansion_id=M4 |title=東京国立博物館 – 展示 法隆寺宝物館 |publisher=Tnm.go.jp |access-date=2012-08-15}} === Kudara Kannon === [[File:Kudara kannon 1.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Kudara Kannon]] Kudara Kannon is one of the best representative Buddhist sculptures in the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, with Guze Kannon in Yumedono. Probably made in the early – middle 7th century, it is 209 cm in height and has a quite slim figure. While frontality is a prominent characteristic of the Shaka Triad by Tori Busshi, this statue intends to be seen rather from a side.小泉 惠英 (1998), "百済観音 (Kudara Kannon)", in 大橋一章 (ed.) "法隆寺美術 論争と視点 (The Art of Horyuji, the perspectives of controversy)", グラフ社, pp.121–150. Most parts of it are made of {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|qid=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}}. Initially some parts, such as its bare body, were finished with {{ill|Lacquer|en|Lacquer|qid=Q11236878|lt=Lacquer|1=Lacquer}}, and colored vividly, but the lacquer has severely degraded over time. The origin of the statue is veiled in mystery. Ancient catalogs for Hōryūji's treasures, even one written in the late Kamakura period, never mention it. The first surviving record which referred to it was from 1698 C.E. and implies that it was moved from somewhere sometime after the Kamakura period. The record calls it Kokūzō Bosatsu ({{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Ākāśagarbha Bodhisattva|1=Ākāśagarbha}}), not Kannon Bosatsu ([[Kannon|Guanyin]]), and says "it came from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], but was made in {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}." At least in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, no official records remained about its origin, which continues to be shrouded in legend. Although it is referred to as the ''Kudara Kannon'' today, its name in the oral tradition was Kokūzō Bosatsu. In 1911, a crown created for the statue was discovered in a storehouse of Hōryūji. The crown was characterized with a curved [[Amitābha]], a typical attribute for Kannon statues. Since then, it has been commonly known to be a depiction of Kannon. The name ''Kudara Kannon'' first appeared in a book published in 1917. While its precise origins are unclear, some modern-day investigations suggest that the statue was made in Japan during the Asuka period.{{cite web|url=https://www.e-theoria.com/museum/042.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210624033449/https://www.e-theoria.com/museum/042.html|script-title=ja:Statue of Kudara Kannon|language=ja|publisher=Kokuyo Co., Ltd|archive-date=24 June 2021|access-date=8 May 2023}} The statue is carved from {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|qid=Q158722|lt=Camphora officinarum|1=Camphora officinarum}} (a species of evergreen tree that is native to China south of the Yangtze River, Taiwan, southern Japan, Korea, India, and Vietnam), which was a very typical medium for Japanese Buddhist sculptures in the 7th century. The base of the statue and the water bottle it holds are made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese hinoki cypress]], a species endemic to Japan.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%99%BE%E6%B8%88%E8%A6%B3%E9%9F%B3-55460|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230508141336/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%99%BE%E6%B8%88%E8%A6%B3%E9%9F%B3-55460|script-title=ja:百済観音|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=8 May 2023|access-date=8 May 2023}} Furthermore, the style of flower ornaments in the crown closely resemble those of Guze Kannon and the Four Devas in the Kondō. The influences on the style could stem from a variety of places. The {{ill|Northern Qi|en|Northern Qi|qid=Q1143524|lt=Northern Qi|1=Northern Qi}}, {{ill|Northern Zhou|en|Northern Zhou|qid=Q551067|lt=Northern Zhou|1=Northern Zhou}} or {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|qid=Q7405|lt=Sui|1=Sui dynasty}} dynasties are all possibilities. The relative rarity of surviving Chinese Buddhist sculptures makes it difficult to pinpoint the potential influences on the sculpture from China. === Murals === [[File:第06号壁.阿弥陀净土图.jpg|thumb|Amitabha paradise before fire]] The murals of the kondō comprise fifty walls; four larger walls, eight mid-sized walls and thirty-eight small wall areas inside the building. The original murals were removed after the fire incident in 1949 and are kept in a non-public treasure house. Twenty small wall paintings, unscathed from the 1949 fire, are in their original places while reproductions replaced the parts that were removed due to damage. It is generally believed that the paintings on the large walls represent the {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|qid=Q732344|lt=Pure Land|1=Pure Land}} ({{Lang|ja|浄土}} {{Lang|ja-latn|jōdo}}) with [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka]], [[Amitābha|Amida]], [[Maitreya|Miroku]] and [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]] Buddhas. Some of the artistic choices, including the way the robes are modeled, are similar to murals found in {{ill|Ajanta Caves|en|Ajanta Caves|qid=Q184427|lt=Ajanta Caves|1=Ajanta Caves}} (India) or {{ill|Dunhuang|en|Dunhuang|qid=Q319114|lt=Dunhuang|1=Dunhuang}} (China). Also observed are Tang and Indian flavors of the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bosatsu|1=Bodhisattva}} and [[Kannon|Kannon]] drawn on the sides of the Amida. Judging from the early Tang influences, the present day consensus on the time of creation of the paintings is end of the 7th century. Thus ruling out the possibilities of authorship by those early 7th-century figures who were once believed to be the creator, e.g. Tori or [[:en:Donchō|Donchō]], there is no clear or accepted author of this work.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=e1BzL2lwPqEC&q=koguryo+monk+murals&pg=PA36|title=Korea: A Religious History "Early Korea" p36|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Routledge|year=2002|author=James Huntley Grayson|isbn=9780700716050}}{{cite book | last = Takada | first = Ryoshin | title = ''Horyu-ji no nazo'' 法隆寺の謎 Mysteries of Horyu-ji | publisher = Shogakukan | year = 1998 | pages = 131–132 | isbn = 978-4-09-387265-2 }} === Shaka Triad === {{ill|Tori Busshi|en|Tori Busshi|qid=Q2579775|lt=Tori Busshi|1=Tori Busshi}} is credited with the casting of this massive Buddhist statue. It is a triad and so [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Sakyamuni]], the center Buddha, is attended by two other figures, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} to its right and [[Amitābha]] to its left. The statues are dated to 623 and the style originates in {{ill|Northern Wei|en|Northern Wei|qid=Q875305|lt=Northern Wei|1=Northern Wei}} art.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zMC4RMXQkn0C&q=chuguji+miroku&pg=PA42|title=Discovering the Arts of Japan: A Historical Overview, p42|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Kodansha International|year=2003|author1=Tsuneko S. Sadao |author2=Stephanie Wada |isbn=9784770029393}}{{Cite book | title=Temple Treasures of Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=s_S4NqCg4iMC&pg=PA15| page = 15 |publisher=Kessinger Publishing|year=2005|first = Garrett | last = Chatfield Pier | isbn = 978-1-4179-6569-4}} The style of the statue is also known as Tori style and is characterized by the two-dimensionality of the figure and the repetitive pattern-like depictions of the cloth the triad sits upon. At each corner of the triad stand four wooden [[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]] statues from the end of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}. They are the oldest examples of Shitennō statues in Japan.{{cite book | title=''Hōryūji'' (tourist brochure)| year=2007| pages=11–12| publisher=Benrido Co., Ltd.| location=Kyoto, Japan}} === Tamamushi Shrine === [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|150px|The {{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|qid=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}, ''Tamamushi-no-zushi'', a miniature shrine once decorated with the iridescent wings of the ''tamamushi'' beetle (''{{ill|Chrysochroa fulgidissima|en|Chrysochroa fulgidissima|qid=Q1952770|lt=Chrysochroa fulgidissima|1=Chrysochroa fulgidissima}}'')]] {{Further|Tamamushi Shrine}} The {{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|qid=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}} is a small wooden Buddhist shrine that is currently stored in the Great Treasure Repository of Hōryū-ji. The shrine is named for the many wings of the [[:en:tamamushi|tamamushi beetle]] that once adorned it but have since deteriorated.{{cite book |title=Asuka Buddhist Art: Horyuji |author=Mizuno Seiichi |publisher=[[Weatherhill]] |year=1974 |pages=40–52}}{{cite web |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sentaibutsu.htm |title=Sentaibutsu |publisher=[[Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]] |access-date=9 March 2012}} The shrine was likely created before the year 693, perhaps even dating back to before the construction of Hōryū-ji. It is notable for its smallness (it measures only 226 centimeters in height) and for the fact that it is the oldest surviving shrine of its kind in East Asia.{{cite journal |last1=Walley |first1=Akiko |title=Flowers of Compassion: The Tamamushi Shrine and the Nature of Devotion in Seventh-Century Japan |journal=Artibus Asiae |date=2012 |volume=72 |issue=2 |page=269 |jstor=24240761}} The paintings that cover building and dais are of {{ill|Jataka tales|en|Jataka tales|qid=Q668439|lt=Jataka tales|1=Jataka tales}}, {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}s, the [[Four Heavenly Kings]], and other buddhist iconography. The front panels of the building show the Four Guardian Kings clad in armor with long flowing scarves. On the side doors are bodhisattvas holding [[:en:Padma (attribute)|lotus blossoms]] and forming a ''{{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=Mudra|1=Mudra}}''. On the back is represented a sacred landscape with heights topped with {{ill|Pagoda|en|Pagoda|qid=Q199451|lt=Pagoda|1=Pagoda}}s. The dais shows on its front {{ill|Śarīra|en|Śarīra|qid=Q1156436|lt=relics|1=Śarīra}}, seated monks making offerings, and ''{{ill|Apsara|en|Apsara|qid=Q148773|lt=Apsara|1=Apsara}}''. On the back is depicted Mount [[:en:Sumeru|Sumeru]]. The right side shows a scene from the ''[[:en:Mahayana Mahaparinirvana Sutra|Nirvana Sutra]]'', where the Buddha offers his life in return for more of the sacred teachings, before being caught in his plummet by {{ill|Indra|en|Indra|qid=Q128335|lt=Indra|1=Indra}}. On the left panel of the dais is a scene from the ''{{ill|Golden Light Sutra|en|Golden Light Sutra|qid=Q391694|lt=Golden Light Sutra|1=Golden Light Sutra}}'' of a bodhisattva removing his upper garments before casting himself from a cliff to feed a hungry {{ill|Tiger|en|Tiger|qid=Q19939|lt=tigress|1=Tiger}} and her cubs. === Yakushi Nyorai === The statue of Yakushi of the original temple was saved during the fire of 670.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pO6ErY9hE7gC&q=horyuji&pg=PA300|title=The Art and Architecture of Japan, p300|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Yale University Press|year=1981|author=Robert Treat Paine|isbn=978-0300053333}} While the temple was being rebuilt the Shaka Triad was commissioned or had been already cast. === Yumedono (Guze) Kannon === This Kannon is a statue that supposedly is the representation of Prince Shotoku. It is approximate six feet and one inch and some sources believe that Shotoku was that height. It is 197 centimeters in height. The kannon is made of gilded wood. It is suggested that the statue was made to assuage the dead prince's spirit based on the fact that the halo was attached to the statue by a nail driven through the head. The statue bears a close resemblance to extant portraiture of the prince. The Kannon retains most of its gilt. It is in superb condition because it was kept in the Dream Hall and wrapped in five hundred meters of cloth and never viewed in sunlight. The statue was held to be sacred and was never seen until it was unwrapped at the demand of {{ill|Ernest Fenollosa|en|Ernest Fenollosa|qid=Q933253|lt=Ernest Fenollosa|1=Ernest Fenollosa}}, who was charged by the Japanese government to catalogue the art of the state and later became a curator at the Boston Museum of Fine Art.{{cite book | last = Kinoshita| first = June|author2=Nicholas Palevsky| title = Gateway to Japan| publisher = Kodansha| year = 1998| pages = 587–588| isbn = 978-4-7700-2018-5 }} Art historians suggest that this figure is based on the {{ill|Tori Busshi|en|Tori Busshi|qid=Q2579775|lt=Tori Busshi|1=Tori Busshi}} style. [[File:Inscription on the halo of the statue of Bhaisajyaguru.jpg|thumb|120px|left|Inscription on the halo of Bhaisajyaguru]] [[File:Falongsibeiye.png|thumb|400px|A replica of The Heart Sutra and Uṣṇīṣa Vijaya Dhāraṇī Sūtra manuscript in [[:en:Siddham script|Siddham script]] on {{ill|Arecaceae|en|Arecaceae|qid=Q14080|lt=palm|1=Arecaceae}}-leaf in 609 CE. First page and the first line of second page is The Heart Sutra second page is Uṣṇīṣa Vijaya Dhāraṇī Sūtra Hōryū-ji, Japan. The last line is a complete Sanskrit syllabary in Siddhaṃ script]] == Serving the needs of architectural research == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' records the arrival of a carpenter and a buddhist sculptor in 577, along with the monks, from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to Japan in order to build temples locally. These experts are recorded to have stationed in Naniwa, or present-day [[:en:Osaka, Osaka|Osaka]], where the [[Shitennō-ji]] was built.{{cite web|url=http://www.orientalarchitecture.com/nara/horyujiindex.htm |title=Asian Historical Architecture, Horyuji Temple |access-date=2007-04-02 |website=www.orientalarchitecture.com }} There is no record, on the other hand, as to who exactly were the people that have engaged in the construction of Hōryū-ji, although the ''Nihon Shoki'' records the existence of 46 temples in 624.{{cite book| title=世界文化遺産法隆寺を語る ''sekai bunka isan Horyuji o kataru'' (Horyuji, a World Heritage described)| url=http://www.yanagiharashoten.co.jp/| last=Ryoshin Takada| year=2007| pages=76–77| publisher=Yanagihara Shoten| access-date=2010-07-12| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080416195237/http://www.yanagiharashoten.co.jp/| archive-date=2008-04-16| url-status=dead}} The bracket work of Hōryū-ji resembles that of the partial remainder of a miniature Baekje gilt bronze pagoda.{{cite web|url=http://www.indiana.edu/~easc/resources/korea_slides/architecture/3-2.htm|title=Audio/Slide Program for Use in Korean Studies, ARCHITECTURE, Indiana University|access-date=2007-04-03|publisher=Indiana University|author=Shin Young-hoon |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070313185009/http://www.indiana.edu/~easc/resources/korea_slides/architecture/3-2.htm |archive-date = 2007-03-13}} ==Gallery== image:Horyu-ji28s3200.jpg|Kudarakan'nondō image:Horyu-ji08s3200.jpg|Kondō and pagoda image:Horyu-ji43s3200.jpg|Bronze lantern image:Horyu-ji35s3200.jpg|''Yumedono'' roof decoration image:Horyu-ji15s3200.jpg|Guardian statue image:Horyuji-L0344.jpg| image:Horyu-ji34s3200.jpg|''Reidō and the Cloister of Toin'' image:Horyu-ji19s3200.jpg| image:Horyu-ji onigawara.JPG|''{{ill|Onigawara|en|Onigawara|qid=Q1895128|lt=Onigawara|1=Onigawara}}'' roof tile image:Horyuji Temple 02.JPG|Dragon ornaments on the roof image:HoryujiGrounds0311Print.jpg|The Horyu-ji complex image:Horyuji Temple 01 b.jpg|Panoramic view ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Religion|Architecture}} *{{ill|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|en|Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|qid=Q1333799|lt=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area|1=Buddhist Monuments in the Hōryū-ji Area}} *{{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} *[[Hokki-ji]] *{{ill|Japanese architecture|en|Japanese architecture|qid=Q1422874|lt=Japanese architecture|1=Japanese architecture}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|qid=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|qid=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|List of tallest structures built before the 20th century|en|List of tallest structures built before the 20th century|qid=Q4178424|lt=List of tallest structures built before the 20th century|1=List of tallest structures built before the 20th century}} *{{ill|Masaoka Shiki|en|Masaoka Shiki|qid=Q315152|lt=Masaoka Shiki|1=Masaoka Shiki}} *{{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|qid=Q1131210|lt=Nanto Shichi Daiji|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, Seven Great Temples of Nanto. *{{ill|Tourism in Japan|en|Tourism in Japan|qid=Q1060220|lt=Tourism in Japan|1=Tourism in Japan}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== {{Commons and Category|Horyuji|Hōryū-ji}}
